title: SEGMENT 7 — Phage therapy in the East: Eliava, Tbilisi, and the Soviet tradition

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Phage therapy in the East: Eliava, Tbilisi, and the Soviet tradition
We left d'Hérelle in Segment 4 traveling the world, treating dysentery and cholera and plague patients with phage preparations, with mixed results he was not very careful about quantifying.

By the late 1920s, d'Hérelle had concluded that the most promising place to develop phage therapy systematically was the Soviet Union, in collaboration with a Georgian microbiologist named Giorgi Eliava.

Eliava had spent time at the Pasteur Institute in Paris in the early 1920s, had become a friend and admirer of d'Hérelle's, and had returned to Tbilisi, the capital of Soviet Georgia, with the conviction that phage therapy could become the cornerstone of public health in the Soviet bloc.
In 1923, Eliava founded what is now called the Eliava Institute of Bacteriophage, Microbiology and Virology in Tbilisi, and d'Hérelle joined him there for stretches of time in the 1930s.
The Eliava Institute became the world's largest phage research and production center.
They isolated phages against essentially every clinically relevant bacterium they could get their hands on.
They built up huge libraries — collections of thousands of characterized phage strains, matched against thousands of bacterial strains.
They developed methods for producing phage preparations at industrial scale, in liquid form, in tablets, in topical ointments.
They distributed phage products through the Soviet pharmaceutical system.

By the 1940s, you could walk into a Soviet pharmacy and buy phage preparations against Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, Pseudomonas, E. coli, Salmonella, Shigella, Klebsiella, and several others, sometimes as single-phage products and sometimes as standardized cocktails.
The story would be more uniformly triumphant except that Eliava himself was arrested by Stalin's NKVD in 1937, accused of being a "people's enemy," and executed.
D'Hérelle, who had been in Tbilisi just two years before, lost his closest collaborator and most of his political cover in the Soviet system.
He went back to France.
The Eliava Institute survived — somewhat miraculously — under new leadership, and it kept producing phage products through the Soviet era, all the way to the present day.
Today it's a tourist attraction as well as a working clinic.

You can fly to Tbilisi, present your antibiotic-resistant infection, have it cultured, have phages matched against it from the institute's library, and receive a customized phage cocktail prepared specifically for your bug.
Patients have been doing this for decades — Eastern European patients routinely, Western "phage tourists" increasingly over the last twenty years.

The Soviet and Polish phage tradition, which also produced a major center at the Hirszfeld Institute in Wrocław, Poland, developed methods and accumulated clinical experience that the rest of the world simply ignored for most of the twentieth century.
They published in Russian and Polish, in journals the West didn't read.
They used clinical methodologies that were not designed to satisfy the randomized-controlled-trial standards developing in the United States.
So when American and Western European clinicians considered phage therapy, they tended to dismiss it as folk medicine — interesting, possibly real, certainly not evidence-based.
That dismissal lasted until roughly the 2010s, when the antibiotic resistance crisis forced a re-examination.
We'll get there.
But for now, hold this picture.
From the 1920s through the 1990s, phage therapy was a routine part of medicine in the Soviet Union and in Poland.
It never died in the East.
Patients walked into pharmacies and bought phages.
Doctors prescribed phage cocktails for skin infections, urinary tract infections, surgical infections.
The expertise accumulated continuously.
The phage libraries were maintained.

When the West eventually decided, in the 2010s, that phage therapy might be worth another look, the Eliava Institute and its Polish counterpart had been doing it without interruption for almost a hundred years.
The center of gravity for clinical phage knowledge was, and to a significant degree still is, Tbilisi.
